sentences.text: Yes.
By the Germans.
Then, they took him to the depot, which was called Umschlagplatz, to-- where they collect all the Jews.
And if the-- they were like sardines in a box, and nowhere to go, even to the toilet, or-- or have a drink of water.
Nothing at all.
And at the latest stages, which it was the latest stage of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, of the actions.
They-- Around the depot, they made three rows of Jewish policemen, Polish policemen-- or Ukranians-- and then the Germans.
Because my father told me.
I didn't see the Umschlagplatz, no.
I didn't see it.
So my father was taken there, with the lost eye-- was bleeding all over.
And he tried to wipe, because he was bald.
He-- he was trying to wipe the blood off his face.
And it took him a few hours just to look around.
He didn't do anything, just look around-- go from one place to another and watch.
Afterwards, he told us.
And he noticed a man who was close to the gate, and he was very nervous, and he didn't leave that place.
So he approached him.
That wasn't a very nice thing, but it was the only one which he could survive.
It was problematic if he would survive.
But I tell you about it.
When my father heard it, he said it, look, help me.
I've got wife and children, and the whole family depends on me.
Help me.
He said, I can't.
I don't even know if he will help me.
In that case, my father was sort of-- he was desperate.
He held on to the man's arm, and he said, if your brother won't help me, I won't let you out.
He was-- as I said, he was very courageous.
And he had to be tough to survive.
And that was one of the things which I don't think it was a lovely thing to do, but it was the only thing to do if he wanted to survive.
My grandfather offered a few policeman a big sum, of dollars-- like $1,000-- to get him out.
It was just impossible.
They were the last actions.
There weren't many Jews left, and they refused, because they couldn't, you know, risk-- And when the brother came, he said, look, that man-- he says he's got $50 sewn in.
And the brother said, I don't want any money.
I don't want anything-- I want you.
And he said, he won't let me go if-- if you won't help him.
He went back.
He brought a cap for my father.
Two caps.
And that was a miracle, that they went through and no one stopped them.
Because if they-- that was at night.
If they would stop only my father, they would say, what's going on?
We were crying.
We were--
No, it was after the action.
We were-- we were-- we were sure-- said, this is the end of my father.
We were still living there.
That was our quarters.
In the timber yard, sleeping on the timber, you know.
And we had already the hiding place, but my mother was desperate.
And that was another thing about her.
She had something similar that she was called.
Not me, and not my brother, because we were always protected.
You see, we were dependent, we were protected.
We couldn't do anything on our own.
That's how it was in the-- in the sort of better off families.
Not in the poor families.
Someone--
For instance-- before-- ah, how they-- they had in dollars, mostly.
In dollars.
Sewn in.
On their body or-- or-- you know, rings, or something.
Yes.
I forgot to tell you that my mother had an American bank.
My mother didn't believe in-- in presents like like, you know, rings, or jewelry.
She didn't like it.
She was from a poor, quite-- I wouldn't say poor, but poorer background.

And she believed in money, or in-- sort of-- she got some money, she bought a block of [? lead. ?] Did you-- did you hear about another woman who would get money for her birthday, and buy-- or anniversary-- and buy a block of-- give a deposit for a block of [? lead? ?]
That was my mother.
Or, she also believed in American bank, which was one in-- in Warsaw.
And she used to collect money and put it, because she-- she was, herself, she was-- I wouldn't say mean, she wasn't mean.
But she was brought up in that way that she looked after the money, you know?
She wasn't just going and getting rid of the money.
In an American bank.
Now, when the Germans-- until she understood that she has to take it out, she didn't have time.
The Germans put their hand on it and paid her in a few pennies, and make her to sign that she got the money back.
Because you asked me about the money, so I just recalled, it's-- I'm talking about a later time, but you reminded me of that incident.
So what was your question?
Actually, we didn't have so much money.
But grandfather had, because grandfather collected everything.
It was a-- a company, and-- and the grandfather was a head figure who never worked, but he kept the money.
And he had the money.
Hidden, hidden, yes.
Hidden, yes, because all the things weren't important.
Only the dollars, or the money-- the Polish money were important.
No, he didn't give us.
But--
--you see, we-- we didn't need much, because as I told you, the food was there.
It wasn't enough, but it was there.
You see, what did we need it for?
It-- it didn't matter how we dressed, you know?
Who?
Protection, in what way?
Ah, that we were looked after.
Not the money-- we were looked after.
We were not independent, you see?
Because we were always looked after.
To the extent that we didn't even know what we possessed.
Or it was never a discussion by the table what we've got and what we haven't got.
It was [? terrible ?] to talk in front of the children.
That's why my father always went to his-- even my mother didn't know much.
It was a man's world, sort of.
Yes.
And when we resigned from my father coming back, he come back.
And that was the biggest miracle of my life.
Of course, we were overwhelmed.
We were so happy, and my mother, she was going somewhere.
I don't know whether she worked somewhere else, or something was there.
So it was another sort of weapon-- I just have a look.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Mm.
[INAUDIBLE].
You know, when they catch you-- the catching thing.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Action-- action, it was another action.
She was caught.
She was caught.
It was one of the last ones.
No, I can't recall, but it was--
It was '42. '42, and you see, that place where my father worked, it was [INAUDIBLE], that shop.
And my mother was going somewhere.
I don't know whether to get some milk, or-- I can't recall at this moment.
She was called, but my father told us one thing.
When we were-- and mostly to my mother, he told, because he knew that we wouldn't go far.
But my mother, if she goes out of the shop, she has to be careful to be in the last line, close to the gates, to the houses.
And from that angle, it would be easier to run away.
And that what she did.
That was somewhere in [INAUDIBLE], or some other street where she was caught.
And all the-- all the people were walking, and she tried to be last, and last, and last.
And the German turned around, and she went to the gate.

And it-- it-- what-- what come out is that it was sort of her-- not a fishing village, but, underground, you know, where the cellars are, it was an enormous labyrinth-- you know-- all white-- and it comes out that they were keeping fish, you know, because they weren't any fridges.
So they-- it was cool.
So it was an enormous place, because from that place, before the war, they were sort of sending the fish to all the markets or shops, something like that.
My mother went there, and lie on the ground.
And she stayed there maybe half a day.
She was afraid to come out.
When she wanted to come out, it was already the night, but she couldn't come-- she couldn't find the way.
It was so, you know, rounded in all directions, she couldn't find the way.
Finally, she found it, and that was a second miracle, when she arrived.
And she told us this story.
We were still there.
Then they said that they were liquidating-- ah, a big selection, the biggest selection.
They wanted to make one ghetto, because there were-- there was a big ghetto, and there was a small ghetto.
I think they wanted to-- because there were less and less people, and they wanted to collect people as central as they could to get at them.
And through that selection, my grandfather even went.
They were about six people in a row that were-- I don't remember, or more-- six people.
It was the whole street, and we were sort of holding each other.
And between us, we just helped the grandfather, who was-- he was still looking good, and he was erect.
He wasn't bent or anything, and we went through.
But they were still-- if someone had a a-- back on his-- on his back-- those-- what do you called them?
You know, they were trying with the bayonets, and a few children were hidden, you know, behind.
And we could hear the enormous scream-- Like a backpack?
Backpack, yes.
Backpack.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
I heard an enormous scream-- yelling.
And they were killed on the spot, the children.
And of course, they-- they were saying right and left, and we didn't know what that means, but it came out that we are safe.
So that was the moment that my father said, it's time to leave the ghetto.
We won't-- we won't wait any longer.
And my father said, you're going, to me.
And I said, no, I won't go.
I was such a-- you know, I was so afraid.
I was a coward.
I just have a look at my notes, because the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about-- about June '42.
Or-- or maybe later-- maybe June, July, something like this.
It-- I think it was autumn, sort of.
It was in autumn, I think, but the-- can't recall.
We-- we all left in '42.
So I said, no, I'm not going.
I was a coward.
And also, I had curly hair.
I-- I was telling myself that I looked typically Jewish, which I-- maybe I-- I would be recognized.
Not from the manners, you know, from the way of being, but from my curly hair.
And the eyes-- the eyes were very sad, you know, as usual.
So-- so my father said, all right, so-- Henry-- Richard, you're going.
That's my brother.
Yeah, I remember he was 9 years old.
And he gave him a backpack, and they gave him one [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] like this-- one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
How do you say--
Diamond.
One diamond, and-- and sew it in.
Sew it in.
And my father was thinking, he had a list already.
He was thinking about where to send his son.
So it was sort of a businessman, who was in the sign trade.
Polish, but from a German descendent.
And his name was-- because I have a few who helped us-- his name was Mr.
Freund.
He had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was making some furniture or something, you know?
And he always take-- took the timber from my father.
And my father knew that he's an elderly man who hasn't got any children, and he's got a young wife, and they probably want that child.
And that my father-- my mother was the [LAUGHS] educated person.
She wrote a beautiful letter to them, said, we probably won't survive.
I want you to adopt my son, who is a nice boy, and he'll do whatever you want him to, and that sort of letter-- they sent with him.
And my brother went by tram.
Pardon?
Where we were living.
And then, you know, from that street-- well, I don't remember, it was [INAUDIBLE] or some other street in [?
Krakow. ?] He knew [?
Krakow ?] because we used to live there.
And he-- he asked, because he was a typical [YIDDISH], you know.

But being on the tram, he wasn't careful enough, and-- and the-- his friends, in a-- in a good faith, his school friends, were yelling, [INAUDIBLE], what are you doing here-- like by the name, but a softer name.
They were calling him, and everyone could hear.
You know?
So he already was careful.
You see, for-- for instance, my brother wasn't-- was such an Aryan type that in the ghetto, he got hit by a German.
What are you doing here?
You go out of the ghetto and quick.
You see, he-- he didn't look-- So your brother was accepted, was adopted by this family?
So then, I'm-- I'm coming to it.
He went there, and they took him.
They took him, yes.
He was hidden in the factory, and he was there to the end.
But what an end.
And then my mother left, because I still didn't want to-- I-- I didn't feel secure.
If my parents wouldn't be there, I didn't want to go.
So my mother left, and my mother had another address, where there were poor people-- also clients.
They all were my father's clients.
And my father knew that they hadn't got anything to live from.
They had their own house, but the times were very hard.
And she arrived there, and the woman said, if Mr.
Strosberg sent her treasure, we have to keep it.
This-- those words.
So my mother was there.
So then I went.
I went-- it was-- from the big ghetto, it was a-- a big wall.
It was in-- late in the evening.
You have to go through the-- on the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, [POLISH].
You know, what you climb.
Ladder.
You see, such--
Terrible.
On the ladder, on the top was where pieces of glass jump.
And then, it was no man's land.
If a German saw someone, kill on the spot.
He killed whoever it was, whether it was a person, or an animal, he killed on the spot.
So I had to run.
I had directions, and had to run to the other ghetto.
Then, over there, I-- I slept a few hours, and it was still-- it will still late, but it was some [INAUDIBLE], they call it.
A few people who work on the [INAUDIBLE] side.
And they-- this why I went with them, but it was-- it was very interesting how it-- it was about 50 people, and four in a row.
The first-- it-- it was a German.
Probably someone was paid.
That what I reckon, a Polish-- a policeman or something.
German wouldn't be, but a Polish policeman, probably.
So it-- a German-- one or two were there, and the Polish policeman.
So when we walk, we walked like this-- from the first row, when the German looked another direction, one of the men disappeared.
So it was an empty space, yes?
Then you--
The second row, someone get-- got, you know, one step forward, and they pushed me to get one step forward.
And I was seen, you see?
So I went to [?
Prague. ?] They were building something.
It was-- they were erecting a big building, the Germans, and the Jews were working as workers.
But by the same time, they were-- they were bringing some valuable things for exchange for food.
And we, the women-- a few women who were working also, they were in the kitchen, like with a caretaker-- it was a caretaker.
And we were-- and for the first time, I had a good meal.
They gave me something to eat, and all of a sudden, the German comes.
And the caretakers said, Germans are coming.
And I lied down on-- under the bed.
But I-- I had long legs, you know, and he noticed me, and others.
And he said, what are you doing here?
And I said, I help in the kitchen.
But, of course, he didn't believe.
I-- you're going to Umschlagplatz, he said straightaway.
And he went to catch us, because it wasn't his duty to do it.
And keep them here, and he went to get other Germans-- to-- to call other Germans to take us.
And it was some force, which I can't understand.
I just-- after him, I just ran, and I kept running.
And a Polish girl was leaving in the next door.
I-- I met her there.
And I went to the last floor, and I was waiting.
I didn't know what to do.
I was on the [INAUDIBLE] side already, with the [INAUDIBLE], which is people who are working on the [INAUDIBLE] side.
And I was caught among others by the Germans.
It was autumn '42.
Autumn-- it was still Autumn '42 when--
Yeah, yeah, because in-- in short distances, we used to-- one after the other.
My-- that was my father's order, to leave the ghetto.
All right.
